skill.name: dispatching-parallel-agents
skill.description: Guides AI coding assistants to split independent debugging or implementation tasks across parallel agents.
skill.summary: Coordinate independent coding investigations across multiple focused agents.
skill.author: Cycleaddict

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns in SKILL.md. Review found these are false positives from Markdown fences, descriptive workflow text, and ordinary debugging guidance; no executable code, network calls, credential handling, or prompt injection attempts were found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported external command locations are Markdown code fences or examples for Graphviz, TypeScript task calls, and agent prompts. They do not execute shell commands or interpolate user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines are visible Markdown fence delimiters or example-only text. No Ruby backtick execution or shell command syntax is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography location is the YAML description line for the skill. It describes task independence and contains no cryptographic API, hash function, cipher, or secret handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is metadata prose, not code. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or any cryptographic operation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: The network and system reconnaissance hits are normal workflow instructions about giving agents clear goals and checking for edit conflicts. They do not enumerate systems, scan networks, or collect host information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both lines are benign checklist items in a debugging workflow. No commands, network targets, or system discovery instructions are present.

content.value_statement: Sequential debugging slows teams when failures are unrelated. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code split independent work into focused parallel agent tasks.
actual_capabilities: Identifies when multiple coding tasks can be investigated independently.
Explains how to create one focused agent task for each problem domain.
Provides prompt structure for scoped debugging and test repair work.
Guides review of returned agent summaries before integration.
Highlights verification steps such as conflict checks and full test runs.
limitations: It is a workflow guide, not an automation script or agent runtime.
It does not decide independence without accurate project context from the user.
It is less useful when failures share state or require full-system reasoning.
It does not guarantee that parallel agent outputs will merge cleanly.

use_cases.title: Split unrelated test failures
use_cases.description: Group failing test files by root cause and assign each group to a separate focused agent.

use_cases.title: Coordinate refactor cleanup
use_cases.description: Break independent post-refactor issues into separate investigation scopes with clear constraints.

use_cases.title: Improve AI coding throughput
use_cases.description: Use multiple agent runs to investigate unrelated bugs while preserving integration review steps.

prompt_templates.title: Find independent tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these failing tests and group them by independent problem domain. Explain which groups can be handled in parallel and why.

prompt_templates.title: Create focused agent prompts
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare scoped instructions for each parallel agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn these independent failures into separate agent tasks. Each task should include scope, goal, constraints, and expected output.

prompt_templates.title: Dispatch debugging work
prompt_templates.scenario: Use parallel agents for unrelated failures.
prompt_templates.prompt: Dispatch one agent per independent failure group. Ask each agent to identify the root cause, make focused fixes, and return a concise summary.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate parallel results
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review after multiple agents return.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these agent summaries and proposed changes. Identify conflicts, integration risks, missing verification, and the safest order to validate the combined result.

output_examples.input: Three test files fail after a refactor, and each file covers a different subsystem.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends one agent per subsystem, with scoped prompts and a final integration review after all agents return.

output_examples.input: Several failures may share one root cause in authentication middleware.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a single investigation first, because parallel agents could duplicate work or make conflicting changes.

output_examples.input: Two agents return fixes that touch the same helper function.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends conflict review, targeted tests for both affected areas, and a full suite run before accepting changes.

best_practices: Dispatch agents only after confirming that tasks have independent context and outputs.
Give each agent a narrow scope, explicit constraints, and a required summary format.
Review all returned changes together before running final verification.
anti_patterns: Do not parallelize failures that likely share one root cause.
Do not give agents broad instructions such as fixing all tests.
Do not merge parallel results without checking for overlapping edits.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps split independent coding failures or tasks across multiple focused AI agents.

faq.question: Does it run agents automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance for structuring and coordinating parallel agent work.

faq.question: When should I avoid using it?
faq.answer: Avoid it when failures are related, require shared context, or may involve the same files.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow is written for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code style coding agents.

faq.question: How should agent prompts be written?
faq.answer: Prompts should include one problem domain, enough context, clear constraints, and expected output.

faq.question: What should happen after agents finish?
faq.answer: Review each summary, check for conflicts, run targeted tests, and then run broader verification.
